title: pH and the hydronium concentration
definition: pH compresses the hydronium concentration onto a log scale: $\mathrm{pH} = -\log_{10}[\mathrm{H^+}]$. Because it is a **logarithm**, every whole pH unit is a **tenfold** change in $[\mathrm{H^+}]$ — pH 3 is ten times more acidic than pH 4. At 25 °C pure water is pH 7 (neutral); below 7 is acidic, above 7 basic, tied together by $[\mathrm{H^+}][\mathrm{OH^-}] = K_w = 10^{-14}$. On a log scale the **decimal places are the significant figures**: a two-significant-figure $[\mathrm{H^+}]$ fixes pH to two decimals. Reading pH backwards, $[\mathrm{H^+}] = 10^{-\mathrm{pH}}$.
